_comment: Protein-family → preferred modality mapping for §2.2 Tractability fallback. Used when Open Targets has no tractability assessment for the target. Based on industry conventional wisdom for what modality has the highest precedent in each family. Hand-curated; refresh ad-hoc when a family's modality landscape shifts.

families.rationale: Active site is well-characterized and ATP-competitive small molecules are the dominant successful modality (e.g., imatinib, gefitinib, ibrutinib).
Largest drugged target class; small-molecule agonists/antagonists/PAMs/NAMs dominate.
Channel pore and allosteric sites are amenable to small-molecule modulators.
Active-site inhibitors are well-precedented; some emerging covalent and PROTAC strategies.
Ligand-binding domain is small-molecule-tractable; SARMs and SERDs are emerging modalities.
Historically considered undruggable but allosteric small molecules and covalent strategies are advancing.
Most TFs lack a druggable pocket; degraders bypass the need for a binding-site small-molecule inhibitor.
Lack catalytic activity; degradation is the primary tractability strategy.
Extracellular target — antibodies are the precedent (e.g., adalimumab, denosumab, omalizumab).
Extracellular epitopes are antibody-accessible; many approved mAbs (e.g., trastuzumab, cetuximab).
Substrate-binding sites are small-molecule-tractable; well-precedented for SSRIs, GLP-1RAs, etc.
Used as PROTAC handles or directly inhibited (e.g., MDM2 inhibitors); molecular glue strategies emerging.
Active sites and reader pockets are small-molecule-tractable (e.g., vorinostat, JQ1-derived BET inhibitors).
Historically intractable; recent KRAS G12C covalent inhibitors and cycle modulators have established precedent.
Polymerization sites are small-molecule-tractable (e.g., taxanes, vinca alkaloids).

default.rationale: Industry default when family is unclassified — small molecules remain the highest-precedent modality across most target classes.
